votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, using integer division which is exact since n*(n+1) is always even.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, using integer division which matches Coq's behavior since n*(n+1) is always non-negative.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, using integer division which matches Coq's Z division since n*(n+1) is always non-negative.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute result == (n * (n + 1)) / 2, where Python's // operator performs integer division matching Coq's Z division semantics for the sum formula. For all concrete integer inputs n and result, both will return the same boolean judgment.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute result == (n * (n + 1)) / 2, where Python's // operator performs integer division matching Coq's Z division semantics for the sum formula. For all concrete integer inputs n and result, both will return the same boolean judgment.
Both check result equals (n*(n+1))/2 using integer division by 2. In Coq, Z.div with positive divisor 2 matches Python //. Also n*(n+1) is always even, so the division is exact for all integers n.
Both define result as (n*(n+1))/2 over integers. Since n*(n+1) is always even, Coq’s Z division by 2 matches Python’s // for all integers, including negatives.
Both check result equals (n*(n+1))/2 over integers; Coq uses Z.div and Python // is the same floor division on unbounded ints.
